color-contrast: Frequently flags false positives for text rendered over CSS gradients, background images, or semi-transparent/translucent overlays. Also triggers incorrectly when text color is inherited through complex cascade rules, when opacity or rgba/hsla values create sufficient contrast that axe-core cannot compute, or when CSS filters, mix-blend-mode, or backdrop-filter alter the effective appearance. Manually verify by inspecting the rendered visual contrast using a tool like the Colour Contrast Analyser or a browser DevTools contrast picker.
image-alt: Flags decorative images that intentionally use alt="" (empty alt attribute), which is the correct technique for images that are purely presentational. Also triggers on CSS background images injected via content or pseudo-elements, and on images whose meaning is conveyed by adjacent text. Verify whether the image is truly decorative, redundant, or already described by surrounding context before treating as a real issue.
aria-allowed-attr: Can flag false positives on custom composite widgets that use valid but non-standard ARIA attribute combinations not yet recognized by the axe-core rule mappings. This includes novel widget patterns using aria-expanded, aria-controls, or aria-activedescendant in ways that are technically conformant to the ARIA spec but fall outside common usage patterns. Verify against the WAI-ARIA specification directly.
duplicate-id-aria: Common false positive in single-page applications (SPAs) and frameworks like React, Angular, or Vue where components are dynamically mounted and unmounted. IDs may appear duplicated in a DOM snapshot but are never simultaneously active or visible. Also triggered by server-side rendering hydration mismatches. Check whether both elements with the duplicate ID are actually present and visible in the live DOM at the same time.
link-in-text-block: Flags links inside text blocks that rely on color alone to be distinguishable. However, this is a false positive when links are visually distinguished by underlines, borders, background changes, font-weight differences, icons, or other non-color visual indicators that axe-core cannot detect via DOM inspection. Manually confirm whether there is a non-color visual differentiator on the links.
aria-hidden-focus: Reports focusable elements inside aria-hidden regions. False positives arise when JavaScript dynamically toggles aria-hidden based on user interaction (e.g., modals, drawers, accordions, tab panels) and the element is not actually focusable at the time aria-hidden is true. Also triggered during animations or transitions where the state is temporarily inconsistent. Test interactively to confirm whether the focusable element is reachable while aria-hidden is active.
region: Flags content that is not contained within an ARIA landmark region. This produces false positives for intentionally supplementary content such as skip-navigation links, decorative elements, inline SVGs, portal/teleport containers used by frameworks, cookie banners, and minor structural wrappers that do not constitute meaningful page regions. Evaluate whether the flagged content genuinely needs to be within a landmark for assistive technology navigation.
heading-order: Flags heading level skips (e.g., jumping from h2 to h4). This can be a false positive in widget contexts, embedded components, card-based layouts, or third-party content where the heading level is contextually appropriate even if it does not form a strict sequential hierarchy. Also common when headings are visually styled to match a design system that does not align with document outline order. Verify whether the heading hierarchy makes logical sense in context.
landmark-one-main: Flags pages that lack a single main landmark or have multiple <main> elements. False positive on pages that intentionally contain multiple primary content areas (e.g., comparison layouts, multi-panel editors, email clients with reading panes), or on pages where main content is injected dynamically after the initial audit. Check whether the page architecture intentionally uses multiple main regions and whether assistive technology navigation is still coherent.
list: Flags elements that axe-core expects to be semantic lists (<ul>, <ol>) but are implemented with divs or other elements styled to look like lists via CSS. This is a false positive when the content is not logically a list (e.g., a set of navigation cards, a tag cloud, or a flexbox layout that visually resembles a list). Determine whether the content genuinely represents a list that would benefit from semantic list markup for screen reader users.
listitem: Flags <li> elements not directly inside <ul>, <ol>, or <menu>, or non-<li> children inside list containers. False positives occur when CSS frameworks or component libraries inject wrapper divs between list containers and items, or when role="list" and role="listitem" are used on non-standard elements. Verify the actual DOM structure and confirm whether the parent-child relationship is intact via ARIA roles.
frame-title: Flags iframes missing a title attribute. Can be a false positive for iframes that are purely technical (tracking pixels, hidden auth frames, analytics embeds) and are set to display:none, have zero dimensions, or use aria-hidden="true". Verify whether the iframe presents content that is reachable or meaningful to assistive technology users.
label: Flags form inputs without associated labels. False positives occur when inputs are labeled using aria-label, aria-labelledby, or the title attribute rather than a visible <label> element. Also triggers on hidden inputs, inputs within fieldsets with descriptive legends, or search inputs with adjacent visible text that axe-core does not associate. Confirm whether the input is programmatically labeled through an alternative mechanism.
meta-viewport: Flags <meta name="viewport"> tags that set maximum-scale or user-scalable=no. While this is a legitimate concern, it produces false positives on pages where pinch-to-zoom is re-implemented via JavaScript, on application-like experiences (e.g., maps, drawing canvases) where zooming interferes with functionality, or when the viewport meta is dynamically updated. Verify whether the user can effectively zoom the content.
aria-required-children: Flags ARIA roles that are missing expected child roles (e.g., a role="list" missing role="listitem" children). False positives occur when child elements are loaded asynchronously, rendered via framework portals, or when the children use implicit ARIA roles from their HTML semantics. Inspect the live DOM to confirm whether required child roles are present after full rendering.
aria-required-parent: Flags elements with ARIA roles that require a specific parent role (e.g., role="listitem" without a role="list" parent). False positives arise in component-based architectures where the parent-child relationship is maintained logically but not in the direct DOM hierarchy, or when ARIA roles are applied across shadow DOM boundaries. Verify the accessibility tree rather than just the DOM tree.
scrollable-region-focusable: Flags scrollable containers that are not keyboard-focusable. False positives occur when the scrollable region contains focusable interactive elements that allow keyboard users to scroll via those elements, or when JavaScript handles keyboard scrolling separately. Test whether keyboard users can actually access and scroll all content within the container.
nested-interactive: Flags interactive elements nested inside other interactive elements (e.g., a button inside a link). False positives occur when the inner interactive element is aria-hidden or has pointer-events:none and is not actually reachable, or when the nesting is in the DOM but visually and functionally the elements are separate due to CSS positioning. Verify whether both elements are genuinely interactive and reachable.
empty-heading: Flags heading elements with no text content. False positives occur when the heading contains an image with alt text, an SVG with a title, a visually hidden span for screen readers, or when content is injected via CSS ::before/::after pseudo-elements. Check whether the heading has an accessible name via any mechanism, not just direct text content.
empty-table-header: Flags <th> elements with no text content. False positives arise when the header cell contains only an icon with alt text, uses aria-label, or represents a column of checkboxes or action buttons where a visual header is intentionally omitted but an accessible name is provided via other means. Confirm whether the table header has an accessible name.
td-has-header: Flags table data cells that are not associated with a header. False positives occur in simple two-column key-value tables, single-column tables, or tables where headers are programmatically associated via scope, headers/id attributes, or aria-labelledby in ways that axe-core does not fully resolve. Verify the actual header-cell associations in the accessibility tree.
bypass: Flags pages that lack a skip-navigation mechanism or heading structure. False positives occur on single-section pages with minimal content, pages where the first focusable element is already the main content, or SPAs where navigation is minimal and a skip link provides no practical benefit. Assess whether the page genuinely has enough repeated content to warrant a skip mechanism.
tabindex: Flags elements with tabindex values greater than 0. While positive tabindex is generally discouraged, false positives can occur in legacy applications or complex widget implementations where explicit tab order is intentional and correct. Verify whether the tab order created by the positive tabindex values is logical and intentional.
autocomplete-valid: Flags inputs with invalid autocomplete attribute values. False positives occur when custom or emerging autocomplete tokens are used that axe-core does not yet recognize, or when autocomplete is intentionally set to an empty or off value for security-sensitive fields. Verify against the HTML specification's current list of valid autocomplete tokens.
target-size: Flags interactive elements with touch target sizes below the recommended minimum (24x24 CSS pixels for WCAG 2.2 Level AA). False positives occur when the element's clickable area is larger than its visual boundary due to padding, when adjacent spacing provides sufficient separation, or when the element is inline within a sentence and the exception for inline targets applies. Measure the actual clickable area including padding and verify spacing.
